scenario: User clicks 'sign out everywhere'

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Other tabs still logged in, other devices unaffected, token still valid server-side

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Just invalidates current session, not all sessions for user, other tabs broken not redirected

v3_senior.code: await api.logoutAll();const bc=new BroadcastChannel('auth');bc.postMessage('logout');// Other tabs listen and redirect
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Server revokes all user sessions, BroadcastChannel notifies tabs, redirect everywhere

v4_staff.code:
// Refresh token rotation: each device has unique refresh token chain. Logout-all invalidates the user's refresh family. Access tokens expire naturally within TTL.
// Frontend: storage event also triggers, double safety.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Refresh token family invalidation, short access TTL means full logout within minutes, audit log of sessions

v5_principal.code:
// Session management as feature: list active sessions (browser+location), revoke individually, suspicious-activity detection (new device alert), recovery flow.
const sessions=useQuery('/sessions');// each shows device, last used, location
v5_principal.the_realization: Logout-all is the tip of session-management. Users want to see and revoke sessions, get alerts on new devices, recover compromised accounts. Treating it as 'clear localStorage' misses the security product.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for production, v5 for high-value

scenario: White-label app: each tenant sets primary color

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Hover/active states broken (one color), accessibility contrast not checked, scattered through codebase

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Shades (hover=darker, disabled=lighter) need to be derived, text contrast unchecked

v3_senior.code:
// Generate scale from base color (chroma-js / culori)
import {oklch,formatCss} from 'culori';const base=oklch(brand);const scale={50:formatCss({...base,l:0.97}),100:...,900:formatCss({...base,l:0.2})};Object.entries(scale).forEach(([k,v])=>setProperty(`--brand-${k}`,v))
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: OKLCH for perceptually uniform shades, full 50-900 scale generated, text contrast checked via WCAG

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Build system aware, edge-rendered with tenant CSS (no FOUC), Tailwind utilities work as normal

v5_principal.code:
// Theme as data: tokens (color/space/type/motion) per tenant, validated, stored, distributed via CDN. Style Dictionary build. Live preview before save.
// Accessibility automated: contrast checker rejects bad combos.
v5_principal.the_realization: White-label color is the wedge. The real feature is theme tokens that scale to fonts, spacing, motion, density. Get the token architecture right, brand color is one of fifty configurable axes.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious customization, v4-v5 for multi-tenant SaaS

scenario: App layout (sidebar, header) shared across pages; preserve state on nav

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Reinvents routing, no URL, no history, no nested routes

v2_mid_level.code: <Routes><Route path='/' element={<Layout/>}><Route index element={<Home/>}/><Route path='settings' element={<Settings/>}/></Route></Routes>
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Layout re-renders on every nav, sidebar state lost, no per-route data fetching

v3_senior.code:
// Memoize sidebar, stable refs, layout doesn't re-render with route
function Layout(){return <><MemoSidebar/><Outlet/></>}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Layout stable across route changes, sidebar state preserved, Outlet swaps page content only

v4_staff.code:
// Parallel routes / nested loaders: page data loads in parallel with layout data. RSC streams them independently.
// React Router 6.4+ defer pattern or TanStack Router parallel routes
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: No waterfalls (layout doesn't block page data), streaming, type-safe params

v5_principal.code:
// File-system routing with co-located loaders, error/loading boundaries, parallel routes. RSC for layout, client for interactive. Edge auth in middleware.
// app/(authed)/dashboard/page.tsx + layout.tsx
v5_principal.the_realization: Routing isn't navigation — it's a data-loading orchestration system. Parallel loads, nested boundaries, streaming, error isolation. Modern routers are mini-frameworks. Hand-rolling routing means losing all of that.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for data-heavy, v5 for SSR

scenario: Show validation errors next to fields

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Single error, no field association, validates on submit only, no async, no schema

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Validate-on-submit only, custom rules per field, no client/server sync

v3_senior.code:
// Zod schema + RHF
const schema=z.object({email:z.string().email(),password:z.string().min(8)});const {register,formState:{errors}}=useForm({resolver:zodResolver(schema),mode:'onBlur'})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Schema-driven, on-blur validation (good UX), types inferred, error messages from schema

v4_staff.code:
// Server validation parity. Send Zod-validatable errors back from API. Map to fields. Async validation (username taken).
const onSubmit=async d=>{try{await api.signup(d)}catch(e){if(e.fieldErrors){Object.entries(e.fieldErrors).forEach(([k,v])=>setError(k,{message:v}))}}}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Client + server validation aligned (often same Zod schema), async checks, error focus management

v5_principal.code:
// Validation as contract. Schema in shared package, used by frontend, backend, mobile. Errors are typed, i18n-keyed, with remediation hints.
// tRPC / GraphQL with validation. Forms become declarative.
v5_principal.the_realization: Form validation is the visible part of a contract between client and server. When the contract is shared (tRPC, Zod), the validation is automatic and consistent. When it's drift, users get inscrutable errors.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for SaaS, v5 for serious apps

scenario: Measure responsiveness of clicks/inputs

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Not capturing presentation delay, not reported, only sampled clicks

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Doesn't compute INP correctly (P98 of all, special rules), no attribution

v3_senior.code: import {onINP} from 'web-vitals/attribution';onINP(({value,attribution})=>{send({inp:value,target:attribution.eventTarget,type:attribution.eventType,delay:attribution.inputDelay,processing:attribution.processingDuration,presentation:attribution.presentationDelay})})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: web-vitals computes per Chrome team spec, attribution breaks down input→processing→presentation phases

v4_staff.code:
// Long Animation Frames API (LoAF) — what blocked the interaction
new PerformanceObserver(list=>{list.getEntries().forEach(loaf=>{if(loaf.blockingDuration>50)send({loaf,scripts:loaf.scripts.map(s=>({name:s.name,duration:s.duration}))})})}).observe({type:'long-animation-frame'})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: LoAF reveals which scripts blocked, attribution to specific code, actionable not vanity

v5_principal.code:
// INP budget per route. CI runs Lighthouse + WebPageTest. Profile-driven regressions. React Compiler eliminates unnecessary renders. Server-side rendering for first paint.
// Engineering culture: every PR shows INP delta.
v5_principal.the_realization: INP is the metric users feel. Slow LCP is forgivable, slow INP makes apps feel broken. Measuring is half — culture (budgets, CI gates, profiling) is the other half. React Compiler matters here more than most engineers realize.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — captures but doesn't compute correctly
when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for actionable insight, v5 for engineering org

scenario: Detect when element enters viewport

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Recreates observer on re-render, ref nullable on first render, no rootMargin/threshold options

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Each component has own observer (50 components = 50 observers), no batching, doesn't unobserve on element change

v3_senior.code:
// Shared observer per (root,rootMargin,threshold) tuple
const observers=new Map();function getObserver(opts){const k=JSON.stringify(opts);if(!observers.has(k))observers.set(k,new IntersectionObserver(...));return observers.get(k)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: One observer per option-set serves all subscribers, much less overhead, callback dispatches per element

v4_staff.code:
// freeze flag — stop observing after first intersection (for lazy load)
useIntersectionObserver(ref,{onIntersect,freeze:true});// Common React 19 pattern: use as ref callback rather than ref+effect
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Performance optimizations (freeze), modern API shape, works with concurrent rendering

v5_principal.the_realization: useIntersectionObserver is often the wrong question. content-visibility, loading=lazy, scroll-driven animations are platform features that replace it for most use cases. JS observers are a 2018 solution.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for behavior, v4-v5 prefer CSS

scenario: User picks image; show preview before submit

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: URL leak (never revoked), no type check, no size check, no rotation correction

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: EXIF orientation wrong, no drag-drop, no paste, no replace gesture

v3_senior.code:
// Use object URL + img with style transform from EXIF, or use createImageBitmap with imageOrientation:'from-image'
const bitmap=await createImageBitmap(file,{imageOrientation:'from-image'});const canvas=document.createElement('canvas');canvas.width=bitmap.width;canvas.height=bitmap.height;canvas.getContext('2d').drawImage(bitmap,0,0)
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: EXIF respected, multiple input methods (drag, paste, click), accessible button label

v4_staff.code:
// Composable: <ImageInput> handles all sources, validation, preview, cropping. Integrates with form library.
<Controller name='avatar' control={control} render={({field})=><ImageInput {...field} aspectRatio={1}/>}/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Form-library-integrated, configurable, accessible, handles all sources uniformly

v5_principal.code:
// Image input as a feature: client-resize, EXIF strip (privacy), crop, filter, multi-upload, drag-reorder. Same UX across product surfaces.
// Pair with Uploadthing / Mux / Cloudinary widget — leverage platforms.
v5_principal.the_realization: Image input is a product surface, not a form field. Privacy (strip GPS), correctness (EXIF), UX (preview, crop, replace), and ergonomics (multiple sources). Most apps ship terrible image UX because they treat it as input[type=file].

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious products, v4-v5 for design systems

scenario: List has no items — what to show

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Doesn't help (now what?), no action, no illustration, doesn't distinguish empty vs filtered-empty

v2_mid_level.code:
{items.length===0&&<>

No items yet

Add your first item to get started

}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Same UI when filter returns empty, doesn't differentiate first-time vs cleared list, no skeleton during load

v3_senior.code:
// Distinguish states: loading, error, empty-never-had-items, empty-filtered
function getState(){if(isLoading)return 'loading';if(error)return 'error';if(items.length>0)return 'list';if(hasFilter)return 'empty-filtered';return 'empty-zero'};return states[getState()]
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Five distinct states with different UX, filtered-empty offers 'clear filter' not 'create item'

v4_staff.code:
// Empty state as design pattern across product. Component contract: <EmptyState variant='zero'|'filtered'|'error' action={...}/>. Consistent illustration system.
<List items={items} EmptyState={InboxEmpty}/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Empty states are first-class component prop, consistency across product, designers can author

v5_principal.code:
// Empty states are onboarding. First-time empty = activation flow. Filtered empty = recovery. Long-time-empty = re-engagement. Track per-state behavior.
// AI suggestions: 'You haven't created any reports. Want to start from a template?'
v5_principal.the_realization: Empty states are the moment users decide if your product is worth their time. They're not 'no data' messages — they're onboarding, activation, recovery. Treating them as edge cases is leaving conversion on the floor.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for design systems, v5 for SaaS

scenario: Delete button → 'Are you sure?'

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Native confirm — un-styleable, blocks, no context, accidental enter-key destruction

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Cancel is default focus (good) but Enter still confirms (bad), no undo pattern, accidental click destroys

v3_senior.code:
// Type-to-confirm for high-stakes
<input placeholder={`Type ${item.name} to confirm`} value={typed}/><Button disabled={typed!==item.name}>Delete</Button>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Type-to-confirm forces deliberation, focus on cancel, escape closes, danger styling

v4_staff.code:
// Undo over confirm. Optimistic delete + 5s toast with undo. Much better UX than friction.
async function delete(){const old=items;setItems(items.filter(i=>i.id!==id));toast('Deleted',{action:{label:'Undo',onClick:()=>setItems(old)}});setTimeout(()=>api.delete(id),5000)}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Recognizes undo > confirm for most cases — Gmail did this in 2008. Lower friction, fewer accidents (undo catches them).

v5_principal.code:
// Action criticality determines pattern: undo (most actions), confirm (medium), type-to-confirm (irreversible), waiting period (account deletion). Match friction to consequence.
// Audit log: who deleted what when. Recovery via restore from trash.
v5_principal.the_realization: 'Are you sure?' is friction without information. Undo is recovery without friction. Type-to-confirm is friction for irreversibles. Match the pattern to the consequence — junior puts confirm on everything.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — custom dialog, asks every time
when_to_use_which_version: v3 for irreversible, v4 for reversible, v5 for product strategy

scenario: Click cell to edit; save on blur or enter

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No undo, no validation, no concurrent edit detection, no keyboard nav, no escape to cancel

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Tab moves out of table not next cell, no validation per cell, no optimistic update

v3_senior.code:
// Tab/arrow nav between cells, optimistic update with rollback, per-cell validation
async function save(){const old=row;setRow({...row,[col]:val});try{await api.update(id,{[col]:val})}catch{setRow(old);toast.error('Saved failed')}}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Grid keyboard nav (arrows + tab), optimistic with rollback, validation, busy state per cell

v4_staff.code:
// Per-cell sync state. Concurrent edit (another user changed). Conflict resolution: last-write-wins, merge, or reject.
if(serverVersion>localVersion){if(confirm('Cell changed by Alice, overwrite?'))save();else refresh()}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Optimistic concurrency with versions, conflict resolution UI, undo stack per cell

v5_principal.code:
// CRDTs for collaborative editing (Yjs). Per-cell awareness (who's editing). Real-time presence. Operational transformation alternative.
const doc=new Y.Doc();const cells=doc.getMap('cells');cells.observe(updates=>setRows(...))
v5_principal.the_realization: Cell editing looks like a form input but is a distributed-state problem in multi-user contexts. CRDTs solve correctness, awareness solves UX. Spreadsheet apps spend years on this.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for solo, v4 for multi-user, v5 for collaborative

scenario: Multiple active filters shown as removable chips

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No keyboard remove (delete key), no aria, no overflow handling, no clear-all

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Backspace in adjacent input removes last chip (or doesn't), overflow scroll/wrap?, no animation

v3_senior.code:
// Backspace on empty input removes last chip (Gmail/Twitter pattern), focus moves to chip
onKeyDown={e=>{if(e.key==='Backspace'&&!value&&chips.length){e.preventDefault();focusLastChip()}}}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Smart backspace, focus management, FLIP animation on remove, wrap layout

v4_staff.code:
// Composable: <Combobox> + <ChipList> + <SearchInput> share state via context. Each piece reusable. URL-synced.
<FilterBar value={filters} onChange={setFilters}>...</FilterBar>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Composable parts, URL state, work as search-with-tags or pure filter, accessible

v5_principal.code:
// Filters as language. 'is:open assignee:me created:>2024-01-01'. Parser → AST → URL → query. Power users autocomplete. Saved views. GitHub search syntax.
const ast=parseFilterQuery('is:open assignee:me');const where=astToSQL(ast)
v5_principal.the_realization: Chips are visual; filters are query language. Expose the language — power users write 'is:open assignee:me', autocomplete suggests fields, chips render the AST. GitHub search is the model.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 design system, v5 power-user product

scenario: User drops folder, all nested files uploaded

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Folder contents missed — files only has top-level

v2_mid_level.code:
// webkitGetAsEntry to recurse
async function traverse(entry,path=''){if(entry.isFile){entry.file(f=>upload(f,path+f.name))}else if(entry.isDirectory){const reader=entry.createReader();reader.readEntries(entries=>entries.forEach(e=>traverse(e,path+entry.name+'/')))}}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: readEntries batches at 100 — need to call until empty. Path lost on server. Massive folder freezes UI.

v3_senior.code:
// Read entries fully
async function readAll(reader){let all=[];let batch;do{batch=await new Promise(r=>reader.readEntries(r));all.push(...batch)}while(batch.length>0);return all}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Recursive read works for large folders, path preserved as upload metadata, progress aggregated

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Modern API where supported, can write back (sync folders), permissions handled

v5_principal.code:
// Folder upload is a sync problem in disguise. File System Observer (incubation) → real-time sync. Service worker manages queue. Background Sync API for offline.
// Treat as Dropbox-lite, not 'upload many files'.
v5_principal.the_realization: Folder upload looks like recursion. It's actually file-system mirroring with offline, sync, conflict resolution. If users drop folders, they probably want sync. Design for that, not for one-shot upload.

where_most_engineers_stop: v1 — folder dropped, top-level uploaded, confused users
when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for sync products

scenario: Click thumbnail → fullscreen with prev/next

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No prev/next, no keyboard, no swipe, no preload, no zoom, no close button

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No swipe, no preload of next/prev, no fullscreen API, blocked focus

v3_senior.code:
// Preload neighbors
useEffect(()=>{new Image().src=images[idx+1];new Image().src=images[idx-1]},[idx]);// Focus trap, restore on close, prefers-reduced-motion
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Preloading, focus management, motion preferences, swipe via touch events

v4_staff.code:
// yet-another-react-lightbox / photoswipe — battle-tested
// Composes plugins: zoom, captions, thumbnails, fullscreen, video
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Leverages library that's solved every edge case, plugin composition, configurable

v5_principal.the_realization: Lightboxes have been solved. Don't write one. Spend the energy on the transition that makes the thumbnail feel like it grew — View Transitions API does this for free, and it's the difference between 'gallery' and 'feels native'.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 library always, v5 for polish

scenario: Drag cards between columns; reorder within column

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No animation, no drop placeholder, no scroll-while-dragging, no keyboard, no touch

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No scroll across columns, no nested drag (subtasks), keyboard support limited

v3_senior.code:
// dnd-kit with sensors and overlay
<DndContext sensors={[useSensor(PointerSensor),useSensor(KeyboardSensor,{coordinateGetter})]}>...<DragOverlay>{activeId&&<Card.../>}</DragOverlay></DndContext>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Keyboard support (full parity), DragOverlay for unconstrained drag preview, accessible announcements

v4_staff.code:
// Fractional indexing for ordering: position between two strings allows insertion without reordering all
import {generateKeyBetween} from 'fractional-indexing';const newPos=generateKeyBetween(prev?.pos,next?.pos)
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Reorder = single update (not N), works under high concurrency, multi-user-safe

v5_principal.code:
// CRDT (Yjs Y.Array) for true concurrent edits. Presence (who's dragging what). Awareness API. Offline-first with sync on reconnect.
const items=ydoc.getArray('items');items.observe(events=>setItems(...))
v5_principal.the_realization: Kanban looks like drag-drop. Real kanban is distributed state: reordering, concurrent moves, presence, offline. Trello/Linear spend years here. dnd-kit handles UI; CRDTs handle correctness.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for serious tools, v5 for collaborative

scenario: Send notifications even when site closed

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Only works while page open, no service worker, no subscription, no server delivery

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Asks permission unprompted (bad UX), no unsubscribe, no preference UI, badge counter not synced

v3_senior.code:
// Permission UX: ask only after user action (clicked 'enable notifications'). Soft-ask before native prompt.
function enableNotifs(){if(Notification.permission==='default'){showSoftPrompt(()=>Notification.requestPermission())}}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Soft-prompt prevents instant-deny, ask in context, manage subscription lifecycle

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Rich notifications (actions, images), focus existing tab pattern, badge API

v5_principal.code:
// Push as multi-channel: in-app, push, email, SMS with smart routing (user online? in-app; offline? push; quiet hours? defer). Preferences per channel per type.
// PWA: Periodic Background Sync, Persistent Notification, Notification Triggers (deprecated but conceptual).
v5_principal.the_realization: Push notifications are part of a delivery system. Routing logic (which channel based on user state), preferences, frequency caps, A/B testing. Sending to wrong channel = users disabling all notifications.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 production, v5 for engagement-driven products

scenario: User enters email, clicks link in email to log in

v1_junior.code:
// Send email with /verify?token=...; on click set cookie
setCookie('session',token)
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Token reused (no single-use), no expiry, link opens in different device than requested, no CSRF protection

v2_mid_level.code:
// Single-use token, 15min expiry, hash stored
const token=crypto.randomUUID();await db.tokens.create({hash:sha256(token),expires:Date.now()+15*60*1000,used:false})
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Cross-device problem unsolved, email apps prefetch link and consume token, no rate limit

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Cross-device safe, prefetch-proof, rate-limited per email, audit log

v4_staff.code:
// PKCE-like: original device generates challenge, link includes verifier, server matches
// + WebAuthn / passkey as upgrade path
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: PKCE prevents intercepted link replay, passkey upgrade encouraged, prevents email-bot attacks

v5_principal.code:
// Passwordless as default with passkey-first, magic-link fallback. Threat model: account takeover via email compromise. Add: device fingerprint, anomaly detection, step-up for sensitive actions.
// Email is not a strong auth factor — design accordingly.
v5_principal.the_realization: Magic links are 'email as auth factor' — and email gets compromised. Treat them as one factor among many: passkey primary, magic-link fallback, step-up for sensitive ops. Engineers who treat magic-link as 'easy auth' build account-takeover vectors.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — works for happy path, exploitable
when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 if no passkey, v5 always think threat model

scenario: User chooses compact vs comfortable spacing

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Hard-coded per component, doesn't propagate, conflicts with component defaults

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Every component checks, scales badly, density only affects padding (not type, spacing scale)

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Spacing scale is themed, components inherit, CSS-only switching, no React re-render

v4_staff.code:
// Density affects spacing, type scale, line-height, component heights — a coordinated set. Token system per density.
// Tailwind v4 @theme with density variants
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Density as full token variant, type and spacing coordinated, accessibility (min touch target preserved)

v5_principal.code:
// Density is one axis of personalization: along with motion (reduce/full), contrast, font size, color blindness mode. Personalization profile = combination of axes.
// Accessibility regulations (EU): personalization is required.
v5_principal.the_realization: Density is part of personalization, which is part of accessibility, which will be regulated. Designing token systems that support multiple personalization axes is foundational, not optional. Single-axis hard-coding is legal risk in 2026.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for design system, v4-v5 for SaaS / regulated

scenario: Open detail modal at /items/123 from list at /items

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No deep link, no back button, can't share URL, refreshing loses modal

v2_mid_level.code:
// Modal listens to URL
const {id}=useParams();<Modal open={!!id}/>
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Full page navigation (list unmounts), no transition, back button works but list scrolls to top

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Modal on top of list when navigating from list, full page on direct visit/refresh, View Transitions

v4_staff.code:
// Same pattern in React Router 6 / TanStack Router via parallel routes
// Pattern: list + outlet for modal slot, route hierarchy reflects UI hierarchy
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Framework-aware, scroll position preserved on list, focus management automatic

v5_principal.code:
// All overlays as routes: modals, drawers, popovers with significant content. URL is single source of UI state. Free back-button, deep linking, history. Pair with View Transitions for native feel.
// Edge case: nested modal-routes work because routes nest.
v5_principal.the_realization: Modal state in React state is technical debt the moment you ship. URL-as-state gives you free deep links, sharing, history, scroll. Engineers reinvent it badly; routers solve it.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — URL syncs, navigation feels weird
when_to_use_which_version: v3 for Next, v4 for React Router, v5 always think this way

scenario: User loses connection mid-action

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Action lost, no retry, no offline indicator, no queue

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: navigator.onLine lies (says online when wifi has no internet), no retry queue, lost data

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Reliable online check, mutation queue, retry on reconnect, optimistic with rollback

v4_staff.code:
// React Query persistQueryClient + offline mutations
import {PersistQueryClientProvider} from '@tanstack/react-query-persist-client';// Mutations persisted to localStorage, retried on reconnect
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Persistence across reload, automatic retry with backoff, mutation deduplication

v5_principal.code:
// Offline-first: service worker caches data, mutations queued in IndexedDB, sync via Background Sync API. CRDTs handle merge conflicts.
await registration.sync.register('sync-mutations')
v5_principal.the_realization: Network errors are common, not exceptional. Designing for offline-first means apps work in elevators, on planes, on flaky networks. Online-only apps frustrate users; sync-aware apps delight them.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — banner, hopes for the best
when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 if React Query, v5 for mobile-first

scenario: Catch when PRs balloon bundle

v1_junior.code: npm run build && check terminal output
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Manual, no per-route breakdown, no historical comparison, no PR check

v2_mid_level.code:
// rollup-plugin-visualizer / vite-bundle-visualizer
// Run locally to see what's big
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Local only, not in CI, no regression alerts

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: CI-enforced budgets, PR comments with size diff, fails build on regression

v4_staff.code:
// bundlejs / bundlemon track historical, per-route budgets, gzip+brotli+raw, package-level alerts
// Alert when @charts/library adds 80KB
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Historical trend, per-route + per-package, alerts on specific dependencies, recommendations

v5_principal.code:
// Performance budgets enforced at multiple layers: bundle size (build), LCP/INP (RUM), Core Web Vitals (CrUX), revenue (analytics). Connect bundle size growth to real user impact.
// PR description shows: bundle +12KB → estimated LCP +50ms → estimated conversion -0.1%
v5_principal.the_realization: Bundle size matters because it affects LCP which affects conversion. Tracking it in isolation misses the chain. Connecting build metrics to user metrics to revenue metrics is what makes the budget stick.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for serious products, v5 for engineering org

scenario: Limit how often a function runs

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No throttle option, no leading/trailing config, no cancel

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Confuses debounce (wait until idle) with throttle (rate-limit) — different use cases

v3_senior.code:
// Distinguish: debounce for search input (wait), throttle for scroll/resize (rate-limit), animation frame for visual updates
function useThrottle<T>(val:T,ms:number){const [v,setV]=useState(val);const lastRef=useRef(0);useEffect(()=>{const now=Date.now();if(now-lastRef.current>=ms){setV(val);lastRef.current=now}else{const t=setTimeout(()=>{setV(val);lastRef.current=Date.now()},ms-(now-lastRef.current));return()=>clearTimeout(t)}},[val,ms]);return v}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Right tool per case, useDeferredValue often better for React-internal scenarios

v4_staff.code:
// React 19: useDeferredValue for stale-while-fresh, useTransition for non-urgent updates. Often beats debounce/throttle.
const deferredQuery=useDeferredValue(query);// Render with stale query while computing new
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: React-native concurrency primitives over manual timing, better UX (no fixed delay)

v5_principal.code: // Pacing strategy depends on what you're optimizing for. Debounce optimizes for total cost. Throttle for consistency. Concurrent React for perceived smoothness. RequestIdleCallback for non-urgent. Choose mechanically, not by habit.
v5_principal.the_realization: Debounce/throttle are timing hacks. Concurrent React, IdleCallback, and useDeferredValue are scheduling primitives. The hack is to use timing where scheduling is right.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for clarity, v4 in React 19, v5 for performance work

scenario: Submit button reflects form state

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Double-click submits twice, no loading state, no disabled-while-invalid

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Disabled is grayed (looks broken), no success feedback, no error retry, lost focus on disable

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: aria-busy not disabled, spinner inline, double-submit guarded, focus preserved

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Form actions + useFormStatus = zero state management, works with Server Actions, progressive enhancement

v5_principal.code:
// Submit states are a sequence: idle → submitting → success/error → idle (with feedback). FLIP animations between states. Optimistic feedback when reversible.
const [state,dispatch]=useReducer(submitReducer,'idle');// idle→submitting→success(2s show check)→idle
v5_principal.the_realization: Submit buttons are micro-flows. Idle is rest; submitting is acknowledgment; success is celebration; error is recovery. Each state has its own UX. Junior just toggles disabled.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 with Server Actions, v5 for polished UX

scenario: Mobile gesture to reload list

v1_junior.code: // Native browser does this for top-level page; but in-app lists need custom
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Doesn't work inside scrollable list, no progress indicator

v2_mid_level.code:
// Listen to touch events at top of scroll
onTouchStart -> onTouchMove (if scrollTop===0 && deltaY > threshold) -> refresh
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Conflicts with regular scroll, no resistance/rubber-banding, no animation

v3_senior.code:
// Pointer events + rubber-band physics
const y=useMotionValue(0);const resistance=v=>v*0.3;onPointerMove:(e)=>{if(scrollTop===0&&deltaY>0)y.set(resistance(deltaY))};onPointerEnd:()=>{if(y.get()>100)refresh();y.set(0)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Native feel via resistance, threshold, animated reset, spinner at threshold

v4_staff.code:
// PullToRefresh component pattern via react-spring/framer-motion, with overscroll-behavior:contain to prevent browser refresh on top-of-page
.list{overscroll-behavior:contain}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: overscroll-behavior prevents conflict with browser, composable, accessible alternative (button)

v5_principal.code:
// Question necessity. Pull-to-refresh = bad UX disguise: it admits the list goes stale. Better: realtime updates, smart background refresh, optimistic updates that don't need refresh.
// If kept: as backup, not primary mechanism.
v5_principal.the_realization: Pull-to-refresh is a fix for stale data. The real fix is data that doesn't go stale: realtime subscriptions, focus-refetch, optimistic mutations. Engineers add P2R when the fix is upstream.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 if needed, v5 redesign data flow instead

scenario: Drawer can snap to 25%, 50%, 100% heights

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: All-or-nothing, can't peek then expand

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No drag, no snap physics, transitions abrupt

v3_senior.code:
// Drag with snap-to-nearest
onDragEnd:(_,info)=>{const target=findClosestSnap(currentY+info.velocity.y*0.2,snapPoints);animate(y,target)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Velocity-aware snapping, smooth animation, snap points configurable

v4_staff.code:
// Vaul library — solves bottom sheets canonically. Apple-style snap, internal scroll coordination, gesture interruption.
<Drawer.Root snapPoints={[0.25,0.5,1]}>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Battle-tested, handles scroll-vs-drag (the hard part), accessible

v5_principal.code:
// Bottom sheet is a navigation modality on mobile. Use for: detail (peek), action (full). Pair with View Transitions for shared-element when sheet transitions to full screen.
// Design system pattern, not one-off.
v5_principal.the_realization: Snap points are physics for one-handed mobile use. The pattern is Apple's contribution to UX (Maps); Vaul brings it to web. Get the physics right, you get an interaction primitive; get it wrong, users fight the UI.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for custom, v4+ use Vaul

scenario: Render 10k rows smoothly

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: 10k rows = slow render, slow scroll, huge memory

v2_mid_level.code:
// Pagination — 100 at a time
const page=items.slice(start,start+100)
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Users want scroll, not pages; can't see all at once

v3_senior.code:
// TanStack Virtual const rv=useVirtualizer({count:items.length,estimateSize:()=>40,overscan:5});
{rv.getVirtualItems().map(v=>
{items[v.index]}
)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Renders only visible (50 of 10k), smooth scroll, configurable overscan

v4_staff.code:
// Variable row heights via measureElement
rv.measureElement(node);// Auto-measures real rendered height, dynamic content works
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Variable heights, dynamic content, sticky headers/columns, horizontal virtualization

v5_principal.code:
// Virtual + server-side: don't fetch 10k rows. Cursor pagination, fetch as user scrolls. Combine with TanStack Query useInfiniteQuery.
// At 100k+: WebGL canvas rendering (DataGrid, AG-Grid Pro). DOM can't handle it.
v5_principal.the_realization: Virtualization fixes rendering; cursor pagination fixes fetching; WebGL fixes both at million-row scale. Engineers stop at one layer and hit the next bottleneck. Plan all three from start when scale demands it.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for 1k-10k, v4 for varied content, v5 for 100k+
